abstract:
Electrochemical propylene epoxidation using water as an oxygen source offers a sustainable alternative to traditional industrial methods that rely on hazardous chlorine or peroxide reagents. In this study, we developed an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst (PdPtOx/C) capable of achieving a Faradaic efficiency of 66±5% for propylene oxide production at 50 mA/cm² under ambient conditions. Systematic characterization reveals that integrating platinum into the palladium oxide lattice stabilizes Pt(II) species, which significantly enhances catalytic activity and selectivity compared to pure metal oxides. 

Kinetic analysis, supported by multi-parameter modeling and probe substrate studies, suggests that epoxidation on PdPtOx/C proceeds through an electrophilic attack by metal-bound peroxo (OO*) intermediates. Furthermore, we demonstrate that this system is compatible with both organic-aqueous blends and fully aqueous electrolytes, the latter facilitated by the use of gas-diffusion electrodes to overcome mass-transport limitations. By establishing a correlation between alkene nucleophilicity and reaction rate, this work identifies electronic descriptors to guide further catalyst design. These findings provide a framework for selective, mediator-free electrochemical oxygen-atom transfer from water, advancing the potential for de-fossilized chemical manufacturing.
